title: Regression to the Mean

content.statement: Extreme results are mostly luck wearing a costume, and luck does not repeat.
content.explainer: When any outcome mixes skill and chance, an unusually high or low result was pushed there partly by a lucky or unlucky swing that will not recur, so the next measurement drifts back toward the average. Rewarding a peak or punishing a trough often 'works' only because the mean was going to reassert itself anyway.
questions: Is your best month evidence you changed something, or just the top of a range that was always going to bounce back down?
When you punished a bad result and it improved, did your intervention work, or would the mean have pulled it up regardless?
How much of the gap between your top and bottom performers is durable skill versus noise that will shrink next period?
sop: Identify the outcome you are reacting to and ask how much of it is influenced by chance versus repeatable skill.
Estimate the long-run mean for that metric across many periods, not just recent ones.
Estimate the correlation (0 to 1) between one period's result and the next (see calculation, page 6).
Compute Expected Next to set a realistic forecast instead of extrapolating the extreme.
Judge interventions against that regressed baseline, not against the peak or trough that triggered them.

definitions.def: The tendency for an extreme measurement to be followed by one closer to the average, because the extreme was partly caused by chance that does not repeat.
A number from 0 to 1 (here) describing how strongly one period's result predicts the next. 1 means fully repeatable; 0 means pure noise.
The long-run average of a metric across many periods.

content.equation_example: Mean 100 + Correlation 0.4 × (Observed 150 − Mean 100) = 100 + 0.4 × 50 = 120. A high correlation keeps the forecast near the standout result (skill repeats); a low correlation drags it back toward 100 (the peak was mostly luck).
content.closing_statement: Humility comes from knowing how much of your best and worst moments were the dice, not the hand.
content.question: Is your best month evidence you changed something, or just the top of a range that was always going to bounce back down?

caption:
Principle Nº 350: Regression to the Mean

Extreme results are mostly luck wearing a costume, and luck does not repeat.

Ask yourself: Is your best month evidence you changed something, or just the top of a range that was always going to bounce back down?
